bibliographic_data.title: A Taxonomy of Programming Languages for Code Generation
bibliographic_data.abstract: The world’s 7,000+ languages vary widely in the availability of resources for NLP, motivating efforts to systematically categorize them by their degree of resourcefulness (Joshi et al., 2020). A similar disparity exists among programming languages (PLs); however, no resource-tier taxonomy has been established for code. As large language models (LLMs) grow increasingly capable of generating code, such a taxonomy becomes essential. To fill this gap, we present the first reproducible PL resource classification, grouping 646 languages into four tiers. We show that only 1.9% of languages (Tier 3, High) account for 74.6% of all tokens in seven major corpora, while 71.7% of languages (Tier 0, Scarce) contribute just 1.0%. Statistical analyses of within-tier inequality, dispersion, and distributional skew confirm that this imbalance is both extreme and systematic. Our results provide a principled framework for dataset curation and tier-aware evaluation of multilingual LLMs.

core_content.novelty_claim: The first large-scale classification of programming languages based on their resource availability for code generation, grouping 646 languages into four tiers.
core_content.key_methodology: Merged and deduplicated seven prominent code datasets to quantify each language’s presence via token counts. Introduced a four-tier classification scheme: high-, medium-, low-, and scarce-resource.
outcomes: A comprehensive empirical analysis of PL distribution across modern code corpora.
A clear, data-driven four-tier classification of resource availability for 646 PLs.
A public release of token counts and tier assignments to facilitate equitable model benchmarking, bias diagnosis, and targeted research into the long tail of PLs.
core_content.full_text_markdown: The advent of large language models trained on code (Code LLMs) has triggered a paradigm shift in software engineering. These models can generate complex code, translate between programming languages, and accelerate software development. However, their capabilities are contingent on their training data, which is heavily skewed toward popular PLs like Python and JavaScript. This imbalance creates a 'digital divide' where resource-rich languages receive superior model support while less common languages lag behind, with downstream implications for fairness and security. To address this gap, the authors present the first large-scale classification of programming languages based on their prevalence in modern code corpora. They merge and deduplicate seven prominent code datasets to quantify each language’s presence via token counts. From this analysis, they introduce a four-tier classification scheme: high-, medium-, low-, and scarce-resource. This work provides a principled framework for dataset curation and tier-aware evaluation of multilingual LLMs.
